questions.question: Explain why standard library functions like printf() should not be called from within a signal handler. What is the recommended alternative?
keyPoints: printf() uses internal locks and static buffers that may be in an inconsistent state when signal arrives
Calling printf() from handler can cause deadlock or buffer corruption
It is not async-signal-safe per POSIX standard
Alternative: Use simple flag setting with volatile sig_atomic_t, or use self-pipe trick
Perform actual I/O in main program loop after handler sets flag

questions.question: What is sig_atomic_t and why must shared variables between main program and signal handlers be declared with both volatile and sig_atomic_t?
keyPoints: sig_atomic_t guarantees atomic read/write operations without interruption
volatile prevents compiler from optimizing away reads/writes by keeping values in registers
Together they ensure: (1) no partial reads/writes occur, (2) values are always read from memory
Without both: race conditions and stale values are possible
Example: volatile sig_atomic_t flag;

questions.question: List three functions that ARE safe to call from a signal handler and three that are NOT safe. Justify your choices.
keyPoints: SAFE: _exit(), signal(), fflush(), write(), atomic operations on sig_atomic_t
NOT SAFE: printf(), malloc(), free(), fprintf(), fopen()
Justification: Async-signal-safe functions do not use static buffers or locks
Non-safe functions may hold locks or use buffers that could be in use by main program
POSIX standard defines the list of async-signal-safe functions

questions.question: What is the self-pipe trick and how does it solve the problem of communicating from signal handlers to main program?
keyPoints: Create a pipe before establishing signal handlers
Signal handler writes a single byte to pipe's write end when signal arrives
Main program uses select()/poll() to monitor pipe's read end
Converts asynchronous signal to synchronous I/O event
Allows safe integration with event-driven programs
Handler only does async-signal-safe write() operation

questions.question: Explain what happens when a system call is interrupted by a signal and returns EINTR. How should a robust program handle this?
keyPoints: Interrupted system calls return -1 with errno set to EINTR
Two handling approaches: (1) Loop restarting the call, (2) Use SA_RESTART flag
SA_RESTART automatically restarts certain system calls (not all)
Example: while((n = read(fd, buf, len)) < 0 && errno == EINTR);
Important for signals like SIGALRM used for timeouts

questions.question: Write a signal-safe pattern for handling SIGINT in a program that needs to do complex cleanup operations (like flushing buffers, closing files, releasing resources) in response to the signal.
keyPoints: Handler should only set a flag: volatile sig_atomic_t shutdown_requested = 0
Handler: void handler(int sig) { shutdown_requested = 1; }
Do NOT perform complex cleanup in handler - it's unsafe
Main program loop checks flag and performs cleanup: while(!shutdown_requested) { /* work */ }
After loop: perform all cleanup (fflush, fclose, free, etc.)
Alternative: use self-pipe trick for more complex event handling
Make sure to reset handler to SIG_DFL before cleanup if needed

questions.question: Compare setjmp/longjmp with sigsetjmp/siglongjmp for use in signal handling. Explain why the latter is preferred when dealing with signals.
keyPoints: setjmp/longjmp does not save/restore signal mask - signals may be left blocked
sigsetjmp(sigbuf, savemask) saves signal mask when savemask != 0
siglongjmp restores the saved signal mask
With setjmp: a signal handler using longjmp may leave signals permanently blocked
sigsetjmp/siglongjmp properly saves/restores signal mask for correct behavior
If siglongjmp jumps out of handler, blocked signals become unblocked properly
Use sigsetjmp with non-zero savemask when signal masks need preservation
